skill.name: xlsx
skill.description: Create, edit, analyze, and validate Excel workbooks with formulas, formatting, pandas workflows, openpyxl workflows, and LibreOffice recalculation support.
skill.summary: Work with XLSX, CSV, and TSV spreadsheets while preserving formulas and validating workbook errors.
skill.author: Cam10001110101

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged subprocess calls, filesystem writes, hardcoded URLs, and blocker keywords. Review found no prompt injection or malicious network behavior, but recalc.py intentionally launches LibreOffice and writes a persistent user-profile macro, so publication should include a security warning.

medium_findings.title: LibreOffice Subprocess Execution on User-Supplied Workbooks
medium_findings.description: recalc.py builds a subprocess command for LibreOffice and passes the user-provided workbook path for formula recalculation. The call uses argument lists with shell execution disabled, which reduces injection risk, but opening untrusted spreadsheets in LibreOffice remains an elevated execution and file-parsing risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess behavior is directly present and receives the workbook path from CLI input. Confidence is high, but the implementation avoids shell=True and appears intended for legitimate spreadsheet recalculation.

medium_findings.title: Persistent LibreOffice Macro Written to User Profile
medium_findings.description: The setup routine writes a StarBasic macro into the LibreOffice user configuration directory under the home profile. The macro content is fixed and used for recalculation, but modifying a hidden application profile creates persistence and trust-boundary concerns for marketplace users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly targets a hidden LibreOffice profile directory and writes Module1.xba. The macro itself is simple and fixed, so this is a medium risk rather than confirmed malware.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Documentation or XML Namespace References
low_findings.description: The URLs in LICENSE.txt are legal terms links, and the URL in recalc.py is an XML namespace inside the LibreOffice macro file. No evidence found of HTTP requests, downloads, callbacks, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are static text and XML metadata, not executable network operations. There are no network client imports or request calls in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Blocks Misclassified as Shell or Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md findings are examples for pandas, openpyxl, and command usage in documentation. They are not executed by the skill at install time, although the instructions do ask users to run recalc.py when formulas are used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line ranges are fenced documentation examples and spreadsheet guidance. The only real execution path is the separate recalc.py script, which is covered in medium findings.

low_findings.title: Blocker Keyword Findings Are Spreadsheet Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The C2, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts map to spreadsheet formulas, formula error names, hardcoded-value guidance, and pandas inspection examples. No evidence found of command-and-control behavior, cryptographic implementation, or system reconnaissance logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged words have ordinary spreadsheet meanings in context. The reviewed file contains no prompt injection language, covert channels, or host enumeration commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: recalc.py invokes soffice, gtimeout, and timeout through subprocess.run to recalculate spreadsheets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct and confirmed. They use list arguments without a shell, so the pattern is risky but not clear command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Profile File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: recalc.py creates a LibreOffice configuration directory and writes Module1.xba under the user home profile.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The home-profile path and write operation are explicit. The content is fixed and purpose-bound, so it does not demonstrate malicious intent by itself.

content.value_statement: Spreadsheet work often loses formulas, formatting, or validation checks during automation. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through reliable XLSX creation, editing, analysis, and recalculation workflows.
actual_capabilities: Create new XLSX workbooks with formulas, formatting, sheets, and column sizing.
Read and analyze Excel, CSV, and TSV data with pandas workflows.
Edit existing workbooks with openpyxl while preserving formulas and formatting.
Recalculate workbook formulas through LibreOffice using the included recalc.py script.
Scan recalculated workbooks for common Excel formula errors and report affected cells.
Apply financial model formatting conventions for inputs, formulas, links, and assumptions.
limitations: Formula recalculation requires LibreOffice and launches external commands on the local system.
The recalculation helper writes a LibreOffice macro into the user profile on first run.
Complex Excel features may not be fully preserved by pandas or openpyxl workflows.
Very large workbooks can be slow to load, recalculate, and scan for formula errors.

use_cases.title: Build a financial model
use_cases.description: Create a workbook with assumptions, formulas, formatting, and validation checks for a finance deliverable.

use_cases.title: Clean and export tabular data
use_cases.description: Load spreadsheet data, inspect columns, transform rows, and export a clean workbook for review.

use_cases.title: Update an existing report
use_cases.description: Modify workbook tabs, preserve formulas, recalculate values, and identify formula errors before delivery.

prompt_templates.title: Create a simple workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an XLSX workbook from this table. Add clear headers, basic number formatting, and a totals row with formulas.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze spreadsheet data
prompt_templates.prompt: Read this spreadsheet, summarize the columns, find missing values, and create a cleaned workbook with a short findings tab.

prompt_templates.title: Edit a formatted workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this workbook with the new data while preserving existing formatting, formulas, sheet names, and template conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a formula model
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this financial model for formula consistency, recalculate all formulas, fix any spreadsheet errors, and document key assumptions.

output_examples.input: A sales CSV with region, product, month, revenue, and cost columns.
output_examples.output: A formatted workbook with raw data, summary tables, margin formulas, and a chart-ready monthly summary sheet.

output_examples.input: An existing budget workbook that needs a new forecast period.
output_examples.output: An updated workbook that preserves the template, extends formulas, recalculates values, and lists any formula errors found.

output_examples.input: A financial model with hardcoded assumptions and broken references.
output_examples.output: A reviewed model with assumptions separated, formulas repaired, error cells identified, and source notes added.

best_practices: Use Excel formulas for calculated values so workbooks remain updateable after delivery.
Test formulas on a small range before applying them across large sheets.
Recalculate and scan formula errors before sharing any workbook that contains formulas.
anti_patterns: Do not save a workbook opened with data_only=True when formulas must be preserved.
Do not hardcode calculated totals, percentages, or ratios that should update with source data.
Do not run the recalculation helper on untrusted workbooks without reviewing the security tradeoffs.

faq.question: What spreadsheet formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: It focuses on XLSX workbooks and also describes workflows for CSV, TSV, XLSM, and related spreadsheet data files.

faq.question: Does it preserve existing workbook formatting?
faq.answer: It recommends openpyxl for edits that need formulas, formatting, sheet structure, and template conventions preserved.

faq.question: Can it recalculate formulas?
faq.answer: Yes. The included recalc.py helper uses LibreOffice to recalculate formulas and then checks common Excel error values.

faq.question: Does it require external software?
faq.answer: Formula recalculation requires LibreOffice. Basic reading, analysis, and workbook editing use Python libraries such as pandas and openpyxl.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use with sensitive spreadsheets?
faq.answer: The audit found no network exfiltration, but the recalculation helper runs external commands and writes a local LibreOffice macro.

faq.question: When should I avoid this skill?
faq.answer: Avoid running the recalculation helper on untrusted files or in environments where user-profile macro changes are not allowed.
